concepts.name: Creation ex nihilo
concepts.description: The doctrine that God created from nothing — is it asserted by Genesis 1:1, or read into it?

concepts.name: Logos
concepts.description: The Word — John's creative principle, heard against Genesis 1's 'and God said'.

concepts.name: The Days of Creation
concepts.description: What kind of 'day' Genesis 1 counts in — the classic testbed for literal vs non-literal reading.

snippets.text: In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.
raises: Does Genesis 1:1 assert creation from nothing, or the ordering of pre-existing chaos?

snippets.text: And the evening and the morning were the first day.
raises: Are the days of Genesis 1 twenty-four hours?

snippets.text: These are the generations of the heavens and of the earth when they were created, in the day that the LORD God made the earth and the heavens.
snippets.note: Genesis 2:4 compresses the whole creation into 'the day' — singular — where chapter 1 counts seven. The tension is kept on the record, not resolved.

links.to: And the evening and the morning were the first day.

snippets.text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.
snippets.note: The opening phrase is Genesis 1:1's — the allusion is the exegesis: John re-reads creation with the Word inside it.

links.to: In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.

snippets.text: And there was evening and there was morning: one day. Twenty-four hours fill up the space of one day.
snippets.note: The literal reading: a day is a day. One #Interprets edge onto the target passage; the dispute with Augustine's reading is argument-diagramming's #Opposes — frameworks compose.

links.to: And the evening and the morning were the first day.

answers: Are the days of Genesis 1 twenty-four hours?

sources.title: Augustine, The Literal Meaning of Genesis
sources.author: Augustine of Hippo

snippets.text: What kind of days these were it is extremely difficult, or perhaps impossible for us to conceive.
snippets.note: A harmonizing reading interprets BOTH sides of the Genesis 1 vs 2:4 tension — and directly opposes Basil's twenty-four-hour reading.

links.to: And the evening and the morning were the first day.
These are the generations of the heavens and of the earth when they were created, in the day that the LORD God made the earth and the heavens.
And there was evening and there was morning: one day. Twenty-four hours fill up the space of one day.

answers: Are the days of Genesis 1 twenty-four hours?

syllabi.title: Reading Genesis 1: A Hermeneutics Starter
syllabi.goal: Learn the hermeneutics framework on one chapter: an allusion (John 1), a kept tension (Gen 1 vs 2:4), and two rival readings of 'the first day' (Basil vs Augustine) that dispute via #Opposes.
order: Augustine, The Literal Meaning of Genesis
